---
title: "Food Face-offs"
description: |
  Get them yums!
date: '2026-04-13'
image: "foodwars.jpg"
categories:
    - Food
format: html
toc: true
lightbox: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  error: false
  message: false
draft: false
---

```{r}
library(here)
source(here("packages.R"))
source(here("functions.R"))

series_filter = 11

data = pollresults(series_filter)
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Orange Juice")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Donuts")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Pizza Style")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Mac & Cheese")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Peanut Butter")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Chocolate Chip Cookies")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Pickles")
```

# How decisive were these results?

Seven face-offs this week, which means seven chances to see who has actual opinions and who's basically flipping a coin.

```{r}
#| label: summary-stats
summary_ffo = summarize_two_option(data)
lopsided = summary_ffo %>% arrange(p_value_50_50)
closest  = summary_ffo %>% arrange(desc(p_value_50_50)) %>% slice(1)
```

**TL;DR:** `r lopsided$question[1]` was the most one-sided fight in the building — `r lopsided$leader[1]` demolished `r lopsided$trailer[1]` `r lopsided$leader_votes[1]`–`r lopsided$trailer_votes[1]` (`r percent(lopsided$leader_share[1],1)`), just barely out-landsliding `r lopsided$question[2]`'s `r lopsided$leader[2]` (`r lopsided$leader_votes[2]`–`r lopsided$trailer_votes[2]`). The most genuine toss-up was `r closest$question[1]`: `r closest$leader[1]` over `r closest$trailer[1]`, `r closest$leader_votes[1]`–`r closest$trailer_votes[1]` (p = `r signif(closest$p_value_50_50[1], 2)`) — statistically indistinguishable from a coin flip.

```{r}
#| label: summary-table
binom_summary_table(summary_ffo)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-leader-ci
#| fig-cap: "Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals, ranked from most lopsided to closest."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5
summary_ffo %>%
  mutate(question = fct_reorder(question, leader_share)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y = question, x = leader_share, color = question)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = ci_lower, xmax = ci_upper), height = 0.15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = 2, color = "gray60") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Leader's share of votes", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

# Do New York pizza people also love a dill pickle?

Both are tangy, no-nonsense, East Coast-coded choices. Let's see how much the crowds could overlap.

```{r}
#| label: overlap-pizza-pickle
ov_ny_dill = overlap_bounds(
  a_total = data %>% filter(question == "Pizza Style", option == "New York") %>% pull(votes),
  N_a     = data %>% filter(question == "Pizza Style") %>% pull(N) %>% unique(),
  b_total = data %>% filter(question == "Pickles", option == "Dill") %>% pull(votes),
  N_b     = data %>% filter(question == "Pickles") %>% pull(N) %>% unique()
)
```

Of the `r ov_ny_dill$m` overlapping voters, somewhere between `r ov_ny_dill$min_overlap` and `r ov_ny_dill$max_overlap` (`r percent(ov_ny_dill$min_overlap/ov_ny_dill$m, 1)`–`r percent(ov_ny_dill$max_overlap/ov_ny_dill$m, 1)`) are Team New York *and* Team Dill, versus about `r round(ov_ny_dill$expected_overlap)` (`r percent(ov_ny_dill$expected_overlap/ov_ny_dill$m, 1)`) expected if the two tastes were unrelated. Both options win so convincingly on their own that the "tangy and no-nonsense" coalition is large no matter how the overlap actually shakes out.
